Module 1
Claude MasterClass — Complete Practical Guide: Interface, Features, Claude Code & Cowork
Module Overview
A
Getting Started with Claude
Account, interface, three models, decision framework, custom instructions.
B
Core Power Features
Extended Thinking, Memory, 1M context window, Custom Styles, Incognito Mode.
C
Advanced Workflows
Projects, Artifacts, Web Search, Deep Research, Connectors, Skills, Vision.
D
Prompt Engineering
Five-part framework, XML tags, meta-prompting, interview-first method.
E
Claude Code
Setup, claude.md, Opus→Sonnet pattern, screenshot debug, plan mode, 5 mistakes.
F
Cowork
Folder setup, context files, Projects, parallel output, scheduled tasks, Dispatch.
Prerequisites
Basic AI chat familiarity; no coding required for most sections
Leads to
Claude API, advanced agent workflows, Claude Code for production
Module Introduction

Claude is a layered platform — chat is the surface; beneath it are model tiers, persistent memory, agentic coding, and autonomous workflow automation. Most users never leave chat, leaving the majority of the platform's value untouched.

This module covers the full stack: from first login through Cowork scheduled tasks, organized so each section builds on the last. Sections E and F are where Claude stops being an assistant and starts executing real work on your machine.

A A. Getting Started with Claude A
A1Account Setup & Interface

Create an account at claude.ai. Free tier includes Sonnet 4.6, web search, memory, and file creation. Pro ($20/mo) adds 5× usage, Opus 4.6, and Cowork. Key interface elements: model selector (top), extended thinking toggle (leave off by default), left sidebar for chat history and Projects. Tip: middle-click "New Chat" to open parallel sessions in separate tabs.

›Free tier is sufficient to evaluate every feature before upgrading. ›Multi-tab via middle-click prevents context loss across parallel sessions.
A2The Three Models

Haiku 4.5 — fastest, cheapest; bulk/simple tasks. Sonnet 4.6 — workhorse for 90% of tasks; 5× cheaper than Opus. Opus 4.6 — deepest reasoning; architectural decisions, complex debugging, multi-agent coordination. Don't waste Opus on simple tasks.

›Haiku: speed & cost. ›Sonnet: default for nearly everything. ›Opus: senior architect — bring in only when Sonnet falls short.
A3Model Decision Framework

Start every task with Sonnet 4.6. Escalate to Opus only if Sonnet's depth is insufficient. Drop to Haiku for bulk/trivial work. In Claude Code this maps to an explicit Opus→Sonnet handoff: Opus plans, Sonnet builds.

›Default: Sonnet. Escalate: Opus. Downgrade: Haiku. Never default to Opus — it depletes limits fast.
A4Custom Instructions

Custom Instructions (avatar → Settings → Profile): a persistent context layer applied to every chat. Include professional context, format preferences (no emoji, no buzzwords), technical stack preferences, and working style. Write once; Claude never needs re-briefing.

›Instructions = what Claude knows about you. Memory = what Claude auto-learns. Both can be active simultaneously.
B B. Core Power Features B
B1Extended Thinking

Extended Thinking — UI toggle; makes Claude reason step-by-step before answering, producing a collapsible thinking section. Use for decisions with real consequences, multi-variable analysis, complex debugging. Skip for simple/quick tasks. Targets ~20% of queries. In Opus 4.7 this is called Adaptive Thinking, triggered by phrases: think carefully, think harder, ultra think, think this through deeply.

›On: high-stakes decisions, multi-variable problems. ›Off: reformatting, summaries, brainstorming. ›Costs more tokens and time — use selectively.
B2Memory

Enable at Settings → Capabilities → Generate memory from chat history. Claude auto-synthesizes facts about you and updates every 24 hours. Foundation: three intentional chats covering work context, communication preferences, and current projects. Practice memory hygiene — audit regularly, delete stale entries, write directly with "remember that I always want X." Past Chats Search (same settings area) makes chat history queryable on demand.

›Free tier. ›3-conversation foundation. ›Audit regularly. ›Memory ≠ Custom Instructions. ›Can import from ChatGPT/Gemini (Settings → Memory → Import).
B31 Million Token Context Window

Opus and Sonnet support 1M tokens ≈ 750,000 words. Retrieval accuracy at context edges: 76% (vs 18% on prior models). Context compaction automatically summarizes old turns as sessions grow, preventing degradation. Pro workflow: upload all reference materials to a Project once so every conversation has perfect context.

›Entire codebases, book series, year of emails fit in one session. ›Context compaction handles long chats silently.
B4Custom Styles

Access via chat + button → New Style. Built-in presets: Normal, Concise, Explanatory, Formal. Upload your own writing samples (PDF, doc, text) to generate a custom style that matches your voice. One-click switching between styles (email · social · technical). Controls how Claude writes; Custom Instructions control what Claude knows.

›Create per use-case: one for email, one for social, one for docs. Both styles and instructions can be active at once.
B5Incognito Mode

Ghost icon (top-right of claude.ai). No history saved, no memory fed, no search indexing. Use for prompt testing, sensitive material, or experiments you don't want in your permanent context.

›Functional equivalent of private browsing — real interaction, no persistent trace.
C C. Advanced Workflows C
C1Projects

Projects — persistent workspaces with their own instructions, file library, and conversation history. Three-document knowledge base per project: project brief (goal, audience), examples (3–4 pieces for tone calibration), constraints (always/never rules). Rule: one project per workflow type. Free plan: 20 files/chat, 30 MB total. Chat naming: date + topic (2026-0624 Q2 plan). Team plans enable shared projects.

›Mixed-workflow projects produce noisy context. Keep them separate. ›Start each project with a "project brief" chat.
C2Artifacts

Artifacts — actual downloadable files (Word, Excel with formulas, PowerPoint, HTML) rendered in a side panel. Iterative revision: click any section → tell Claude what to change → only that section updates. Advanced: persistent storage (20 MB, publish-required), live artifacts (connected to Google Sheets), public sharing with remix. Published artifacts can call Claude API via window.claude.complete — no viewer API key needed. MCP server support for Calendar, Gmail, Slack. Gallery: made-with-claude.com.

›Mindset: stop getting text to copy; start building finished files. ›Storage only works after publishing, not in dev mode.
C3Web Search & Deep Research

Basic web search: all plans; single-query synthesis with clickable citations. Deep Research: Pro+ only; multi-query extended session returning a full report with citations. Specificity is the key variable — name the domain, focus, and desired output structure. Deep Research can combine web + connected service data (Drive, Gmail) in one session.

›Vague prompt → generic overview. Specific prompt → usable deliverable. ›Deep Research shows sources, unlike most AI search.
C4Connectors (MCP)

Connectors (Settings → Connectors): Gmail, Drive, Calendar, Slack, Notion, GitHub, GitLab, Asana, Linear, Stripe, HubSpot, Salesforce, DocuSign, SharePoint, Microsoft 365. Claude never acts without explicit user confirmation — always previews first. Office Add-in (Microsoft Marketplace, included with paid plans) embeds Claude in Excel/Word/PowerPoint/Outlook with shared cross-app context. Excel: explains formulas, traces dependencies, builds pivot tables from plain language.

›Connect only what you need for the current task. Audit periodically. Isolate sensitive systems (banking, prod code).
C5Skills

Skills — skill.md instruction files that Claude auto-activates on matching tasks. Built-ins: Excel, PowerPoint, Word, PDF, front-end design. Custom skills: upload as .zip to Settings → Capabilities → Skills. Sources for pre-built skills: github.com/anthropic/skills · skillsmp.com · Skill Hub Club (7,000+) · ClaudeSkills.info (140 curated).

›Front-end design skill recommended first for non-technical users. ›Build custom skills for any recurring workflow.
C6Vision & Image Analysis

Opus 4.7 image resolution: 2,576px / ≈3.75 MP — sufficient for fine text, architectural drawings, dense spreadsheets, medical results. PDF prompt pattern: 3-level summary (1 sentence · 5 key points with page citations · full-page breakdown) + list questions the document doesn't answer. Claude cannot generate images or video — write the prompt in Claude, generate visuals in a separate image model.

›Gap questions at the end of a PDF prompt surface blind spots you'd otherwise miss.
D D. Prompt Engineering D
D1Five-Part Prompt Framework

For high-stakes prompts, structure five components: Role (specific expert for a specific purpose) · Task (exactly what's needed) · Context (audience, background, prior work) · Constraints (length, tone, what to avoid) · Format (output structure). Casual prompts work for simple tasks; this framework is for when output quality matters.

›Role specificity is the highest-leverage variable — "pricing strategist for solo creators" outperforms "a marketing expert."
D2XML Tags

Anthropic-recommended for complex multi-section prompts. Wrap sections in <task>, <context>, <instructions>, <examples>, <input>, <output_format>, <constraints>. Prevents Claude from treating background context as a request to respond to. Claimed quality improvement: 30–50% on complex prompts.

›Tags separate instruction from data. Critical once prompts span multiple sections or include large background text.
D3Meta-Prompting

Give Claude a rough prompt draft and ask it to rewrite it for maximum quality. Template: "You're a prompt engineering expert for [model]. Rewrite my prompt to get max quality — add role, success criteria, XML structure, output format. If reasoning is needed, add an adaptive thinking trigger. Explain 3 things you changed. Here's my prompt: [paste]." High value for recurring task templates.

›Ask for a 3-point change explanation — it teaches you what matters in prompt structure. Invest once, reuse forever.
D4Interview-First Method

Trigger: "Ask me 5–7 clarifying questions before starting. Don't write anything until I've answered. List all questions at once." Best for strategy work, creative briefs, complex technical problems. Slower to start but eliminates 2–3 correction rounds.

›Trades startup speed for output precision. Most valuable when Claude would otherwise guess at critical context.
E E. Claude Code E
E1What Claude Code Is & Setup

Claude Code — desktop app Code tab (claude.com/load, Mac/Windows). Direct file system access to a chosen folder; Claude reads, edits, and runs files — not a chat about code. Companion: Cursor (free editor, chat panel right + live file view left). Three installs: Node.js · Claude Code · Cursor. Run /init once per project to generate claude.md. Cost: Pro ($20/mo, included) or API (pay-per-token; better once usage patterns are known).

›Without /init, Claude has no memory between sessions. Always run it first.
E2claude.md Memory File

Plain-text file in project root. Claude reads it at every session start. Highest value: negative constraints — what Claude must not do ("don't use default exports," "don't touch schema.prisma," "don't edit /generated"). 3–5 constraints significantly improve first-attempt accuracy. Keep under 200 lines. Global rules: ~/.claude/claude.md.

›Negative constraints outperform positive instructions for preventing bad edits. Write them first.
E3Opus→Sonnet Handoff Pattern

Switch with /model opus and /model sonnet. Opus plans (deeper reasoning, catches edge cases, costs more). Sonnet builds (fast, accurate, cheaper). After Opus produces the plan: switch to Sonnet → "Follow the plan exactly and build it." Skipping Opus on complex projects leads to mid-session restructuring and wasted tokens.

›Pattern: Opus → plan → Sonnet → build. Opus is the consultant; Sonnet is the contractor.
E4Screenshot Debugging

Screenshot a broken UI → drop into Claude Code with a short description ("fix this") → Claude identifies the file and element autonomously, using both the image and the live codebase. No need to specify which element or file. Claude may proactively fix related elements beyond what was pointed at.

›Figma export + "Build me this page" = fastest design-to-code workflow. Eliminates manual CSS debugging.
E5Plan Mode & Routines

Plan Mode: trigger with /plan or "show me the plan first, don't change anything until I confirm." Claude lists all intended file changes before touching anything. Use for any task touching 3+ files. Routines: saved scheduled workflows in the desktop Routines tab — name, instructions, permissions, folder, schedule (manual / hourly / daily / weekdays / weekly). High-value patterns: daily commit reviews, weekly doc drift checks, PR digests, flaky test tracking, monthly dependency audits. Local only — machine must be awake.

›Plan Mode = responsible default for significant changes. Routines = Claude Code running on schedule without you.
E6Five Common Mistakes

1. No claude.md — stateless sessions. Fix: run /init. 2. Vague prompts — ("make it look better"). Fix: specify exact element + desired outcome. 3. Skipping Plan Mode on multi-file tasks. Fix: /plan or Shift+Tab before complex tasks. 4. Ignoring /compact — context fills → Claude loops. Fix: run /compact mid-session. 5. Three failed corrections in same session — poisoned context. Fix: fresh session + clean problem description + minimal relevant code.

›These five mistakes cause 90% of beginner Claude Code frustration. Check them before troubleshooting anything else.
F F. Cowork F
F1What Cowork Is

Cowork — desktop app second tab (Chat · Cowork · Code). Available to all paid subscribers. Completes entire workflows autonomously: reads/writes/deletes local files, coordinates parallel sub-agents, works unsupervised for hours, produces real native files (Excel with formulas, Word, PowerPoint). Key distinction: Chat answers questions; Cowork completes tasks. Use Cowork for: multi-step tasks, local file work, repetitive weekly workflows, tasks combining file ops + online research. Caveat: high token cost — reserve for tasks that justify it.

›Chat = assistant who answers. Cowork = employee who delivers finished work.
F2Folder Structure & Context Files

Create a dedicated folder (e.g., "Cowork Station") with three subfolders: context/ · projects/ · output/. Inside context/, three markdown files: aboutme.md (role, business, audience — 2 paragraphs), brandvoice.md (tone, style, words to use/avoid), workingpreferences.md (document structure, ambiguity handling). Claude reads these at every session start — you never re-explain yourself. Global defaults: Settings → Cowork → Global Instructions.

›5 minutes of setup = hours saved on every subsequent run. Skipping this is the top reason outputs feel generic.
F3Projects in Cowork

Projects in Cowork are not file containers — they are workspaces where instructions, context, and work history accumulate. Create one project per recurring workflow type (client deliverables · content production · admin & finance · weekly reporting). Add task-specific instructions per project. Over repeated runs, Claude builds on previous outputs rather than restarting from scratch.

›Treating Projects as file containers (not workspaces) keeps every run isolated. Use them as living systems, not drop folders.
F4Parallel Processing & Real Output

Cowork decomposes tasks into parallel sub-workflows — multiple files read and written simultaneously. Example: 6 client meeting transcripts → parallel read → Excel trackers (conditional formatting, deadline inference, summary rows) + follow-up emails (under 150 words, references specific discussion points) — 3 minutes vs 2–3 hours manually. Prompts are plain English; no special syntax. [+] Cowork's parallelism is what makes it qualitatively different from Cowork running the same task sequentially in Chat.

›Describe the desired output like briefing a person. Stop asking what AI can generate; ask what work AI can complete.
F5Scheduled Tasks

Cowork sidebar → Schedule → New Task. Set prompt + frequency (manual / hourly / daily / weekdays / weekly). Critical: only runs while machine is awake and Claude desktop is open — local scheduler, not cloud. Keep prompts tight (costs tokens). Always test manually before scheduling. Example: daily 9AM briefing (Gmail + Calendar + Notion → morningbrief.md), Monday 12PM content research (trending AI topics → 3 opportunities → Slack post).

›Scheduled tasks are the real unlock — this is how you go from using AI to having AI work for you.
F6Dispatch & Claude for Chrome

Dispatch: mobile-to-desktop task dispatch. Enable in Cowork mobile → Enable Dispatch. Send task from phone → Claude runs workflow on desktop → push notification on completion. Claude for Chrome: browser extension for Chrome/Edge only (not Brave, Arc, mobile); beta for all paid plans. Unlike a Claude tab, it has access to your real authenticated browser sessions — can navigate, click, fill forms on live sites. Security: if you're logged into your bank, Claude for Chrome can reach it. Never enable for banking or medical portals.

›Dispatch: phone = remote control, desktop = execution engine. ›Chrome extension: real browser access. Handle with care.
Module Summary
  • Model cascade: Sonnet by default → Opus when depth is insufficient → Haiku for bulk/trivial. Never waste Opus on simple tasks.
  • Extended Thinking (~20% of queries) and Memory (3-conversation foundation + hygiene) are the highest leverage-to-effort features on the platform.
  • Projects accumulate context over time — one per workflow type, with a three-document knowledge base; Artifacts produce real files, not text to copy.
  • Prompting craft: five-part framework + XML tags for high-stakes prompts; meta-prompting and interview-first for iterative precision.
  • Claude Code core pattern: /init → claude.md constraints → Opus plan → Sonnet build → /plan before complex tasks → /compact mid-session. Five mistakes (E6) cover 90% of beginner friction.
  • Cowork is infrastructure, not a tool: folder + context files + per-workflow Projects + scheduled tasks turn one-time work into permanent automation.
Claude MasterClass · Module 01 · Complete Practical Guide: Interface, Features, Claude Code & Cowork
